note: Non-legislative South Carolina AI policy developments — agency actions, court rules, Attorney General coalitions, federal moves affecting SC, and institutional reports. Complements bills.json, which tracks legislation.

developments.title: South Carolina State Agencies AI Strategy
developments.summary: The official framework guiding how state agencies evaluate, adopt, and govern AI, built around the "Protect, Promote, Pursue" principles. It established the Center of Excellence and an AI Advisory Group to guide agency-level implementation, and remains the foundational document for SC state-government AI governance.

developments.title: South Carolina names its first Director of Artificial Intelligence
developments.summary: Rich Heimann was named SC's inaugural state AI Director, with a mandate to execute the 2024 SC AI Strategy and lead the Center of Excellence. The appointment set governance precedent for the roughly 60,000 state employees affected by AI adoption.

developments.title: AI Center of Excellence stands up and begins vetting agency use cases
developments.summary: The Department of Administration's Center of Excellence held its first meeting in February 2025 and formally intakes and vets agency AI use cases (privacy, security, risk, compliance, and procurement review) before deployment. By June 2025 it was actively reviewing 29 agency AI proposals and exploring statewide ChatGPT and Microsoft Copilot pilots — the operative procurement-governance mechanism for state AI.

developments.title: SC Supreme Court issues interim policy on generative AI
developments.summary: Chief Justice John Kittredge issued a statewide interim order: only court-approved AI tools may be used by judicial-branch staff; AI may not draft orders, memos, or opinions without direct human oversight; confidential court records may not be processed by AI without express authorization; and attorneys were reminded to verify accuracy and protect client confidentiality under Rule 407. One of the first court-system AI policies in the state, it remains in effect.

developments.title: AI Readiness for the State Courts (NCSC framework)
developments.summary: The National Center for State Courts' 2025 guidance framework — the source document the SC Supreme Court adopted as the basis for its interim AI policy. Useful background for understanding the structure of SC's court-system approach.

developments.title: AG Wilson leads 44-state coalition against predatory AI targeting children
developments.summary: Attorney General Alan Wilson led a 44-state coalition demanding that Anthropic, Meta, OpenAI, Google, and others install guardrails preventing AI chatbots from sexualizing minors or encouraging self-harm, with a deadline for the companies to respond.

developments.title: AG Wilson opposes the proposed federal AI moratorium
developments.summary: Wilson joined a 40-state bipartisan letter opposing a proposed 10-year federal ban on state AI regulation in the budget reconciliation bill, calling it federal overreach and specifically citing SC's AI-generated CSAM law as at risk. The U.S. Senate later voted 99-1 to strip the provision.

developments.title: AG Wilson leads 28-state push on Meta AI child-safety safeguards
developments.summary: Wilson led a 28-state demand that Meta review its safeguards after reports that Meta AI exposed minors to explicit content, setting a response deadline. Part of a sustained multistate AG focus on consumer-facing AI and child safety.

developments.title: House committee formally adds "Artificial Intelligence" to its name
developments.summary: The House Regulations Committee officially became the House Committee on Regulations, Administrative Procedures, Artificial Intelligence and Cybersecurity, institutionalizing AI oversight in the chamber's structure. Chaired by Rep. Jeff Bradley; it was the first state House committee in the nation with formal power to propose AI legislation.

developments.title: "Bradley" AI virtual assistant goes live for citizen services
developments.summary: SC CIO Nathan Hogue launched Bradley, a citizen-facing AI assistant built with Tyler Technologies, allowing residents to interact in plain language for tasks like notary applications and tax payments, bundled with identity management and fraud detection and a human-in-the-loop design. The most visible South Carolina government AI deployment to date.

developments.title: Federal AI preemption executive order and South Carolina pushback
developments.summary: A federal executive order directed the U.S. Department of Justice to create an "AI Litigation Task Force" to challenge state AI laws. SC legislators (led by Rep. Brandon Guffey), the Attorney General, and advocates immediately pushed back, asserting state authority. The order directly affects South Carolina's capacity to regulate AI and is the key federal-vs-state dynamic to watch.

developments.title: SCRA releases South Carolina AI Report and launches the SC AI Consortium Hub
developments.summary: The South Carolina Research Authority published an AI report out of Rep. Bradley's SCRA Summit symposium, covering AI in policy, industry, and workforce, and launched the SC Artificial Intelligence Consortium Hub for ongoing coordination across universities, agencies, and industry.

developments.title: ASCEND Symposium proposes a state-owned AI supercomputer
developments.summary: Rep. Bradley convened the ASCEND Symposium (AI for Statewide Competitiveness, Economic Development, and Next-Generation Services Delivery). Its centerpiece proposal is a state-owned sovereign AI supercomputer shared among universities, agencies, and private partners; MUSC President David Cole endorsed the concept.
developments.source: Spectrum News SC · Journal of Cyber Policy

developments.title: Palmetto Promise: The Challenges of AI — A South Carolina Response
developments.summary: The Palmetto Promise Institute (a conservative, free-market-oriented think tank — noted here for source transparency) published an analysis of three key AI challenges facing the state: cybersecurity risk, AI literacy, and workforce disruption, evaluated against South Carolina's strategy.

developments.title: SC Bar has not issued a formal AI ethics opinion
developments.summary: While the Supreme Court's interim order governs judicial-branch staff, the SC Bar had published only informal articles as of September 2025 — no formal ethics opinion governing attorney use of AI. This leaves a standing policy gap for practicing attorneys and is a likely area for future guidance.
